heroOverview: Epididymo-orchitis is simultaneous inflammation of the epididymis and testis, representing the most common form of acute scrotal infection in men. It accounts for roughly 1 in 1,000 outpatient visits annually and causes more days of absence from work or sport than most other urological infections in adult males. The condition typically begins as epididymitis — ascending infection from the urethra — and spreads to the adjacent testis within days if untreated. In sexually active men under 35, Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae are the dominant pathogens; in older men, Gram-negative enteric organisms predominate. Prompt antibiotic therapy achieves resolution in the majority of cases, but delayed treatment increases the risk of abscess, testicular atrophy, and impaired fertility.

keyStats.prevalence: Epididymo-orchitis affects approximately 1–2 per 1,000 men per year; it is the fifth most common urological diagnosis in men aged 18–50
keyStats.demographics: Sexually active men aged 18–35 and men over 50 with urological structural disease represent the two main age peaks
keyStats.avgAge: Bimodal distribution: peak in sexually active men 19–35, and a second peak after age 50
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated millions of cases annually worldwide; exact global figures are not systematically reported but it mirrors STI prevalence in the under-35 age group

definition: Epididymo-orchitis (ICD-10: N45.3) is acute or subacute inflammation involving both the epididymis and the testicular parenchyma simultaneously. The epididymis, a coiled tubular structure posterior to the testis responsible for sperm maturation and transport, most often becomes infected first via ascending spread of bacteria from the urethra through the vas deferens. When inflammation extends across the epididymo-testicular junction into the testis, the combined syndrome is termed epididymo-orchitis. Causative organisms follow the patient's age and sexual behaviour: Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae predominate in men under 35, while Escherichia coli and other Enterobacteriaceae are most common in men over 35, in those with urinary tract abnormalities, and in immunocompromised patients. Less common causes include tuberculosis, brucellosis, and viral agents. The condition falls under urology and genitourinary medicine and is the most important scrotal pathology to distinguish from testicular torsion, which is a surgical emergency. Untreated or inadequately treated epididymo-orchitis may progress to abscess (3–8% of cases), testicular infarction, chronic scrotal pain, and subfertility.

typesClassification.description: Most common in men under 35; caused by chlamydia or gonorrhea ascending from the urethra; requires dual antibiotic therapy and partner notification.
Caused by enteric Gram-negative bacteria, most common in men over 35 with urinary tract structural disease; treated with fluoroquinolones for 14 days.
Caused by tuberculosis or, rarely, brucellosis; subacute onset with a firm, mildly painful epididymal or testicular mass; requires prolonged antimicrobial therapy.
Amiodarone and certain anti-androgen drugs can cause a sterile epididymal inflammation mimicking infection; recognised by the absence of fever and a negative urine culture.

primarySymptoms: Acute-onset scrotal pain and tenderness that initially localises to the posterior-superior pole where the epididymis lies, then spreads across the whole hemiscrotum.
Visible and palpable scrotal swelling that develops progressively over hours to days as oedema accumulates around the infected structures.
Scrotal erythema and warmth — the overlying skin becomes red and feels hot to the touch within 24–48 hours of symptom onset.
Fever ranging from low-grade (37.5–38°C) to high (above 39°C) depending on the severity of systemic spread.
Dysuria or a burning sensation during urination, reflecting concurrent urethritis from the same ascending infection.
Urethral discharge — clear, mucopurulent, or frankly purulent — visible at the meatus, characteristic of gonococcal or chlamydial urethritis.
Elevated scrotal heaviness or dragging discomfort that persists even when lying still, worsened by walking or standing.
Lower abdominal or groin pain, often occurring as referred pain from the inflamed scrotal contents via the spermatic cord.
Nausea or vomiting when pain is severe or when systemic illness from ascending infection is present.
Urinary frequency and urgency resulting from concurrent bladder irritation or urethral infection.
earlyWarningSigns: A dull aching tenderness localised to the back of the testicle (where the epididymis sits) that gradually worsens over 24–48 hours.
Mild urethral discomfort or a small amount of discharge appearing before scrotal swelling develops.
Low-grade fever and general malaise preceding obvious local signs.
A sense of testicular heaviness in a sexually active man after a new or unprotected sexual contact.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe testicular pain of abrupt onset (minutes, not hours) that must be assessed as testicular torsion until disproven by Doppler ultrasound — a surgical emergency.
Rapidly progressive scrotal swelling with fluctuation suggesting abscess formation requiring urgent surgical review.
High fever (above 39°C) with rigors, hypotension, or confusion indicating sepsis.
Bluish skin discolouration or visible vascular compromise of the scrotal wall.
Failure to improve or worsening of symptoms after 72 hours of appropriate antibiotic therapy.

causes.description: The most common cause in sexually active men under 35 in high-income countries. Chlamydia ascends from a urethral infection through the vas deferens, producing a sub-acute epididymo-orchitis that may be relatively mild early on, delaying presentation.

A frequent co-pathogen with chlamydia; gonococcal epididymo-orchitis tends to produce more pronounced urethral discharge and more rapid scrotal swelling than chlamydial disease, though the two often co-exist and dual therapy covers both.

causes.cause: Enteric Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli, Klebsiella, Proteus)
causes.description: Dominant in men over 35 and in those with prostatic enlargement, urethral stricture, or a history of urological procedures. These organisms reach the epididymis via the urinary tract, often with concurrent bacteriuria.

TB epididymo-orchitis follows haematogenous seeding or direct spread from renal or prostatic TB. It causes an indolent, minimally painful epididymal or testicular mass that can be mistaken for a tumour; requires six months of standard anti-TB therapy.

Cystoscopy, urinary catheter insertion, or transurethral procedures introduce bacteria into the lower urinary tract that can ascend to the epididymis, particularly in men with impaired bladder drainage or pre-existing bacteriuria.

Brucellosis, secondary syphilis, and viral infections (rare) can reach the epididymis and testis via the bloodstream. Brucella species should be considered in men with livestock exposure or consumption of unpasteurised dairy products.

riskFactors.factor: Unprotected sex with a new or multiple partners
riskFactors.description: The primary risk factor for chlamydial and gonococcal epididymo-orchitis; consistent condom use reduces transmission risk by approximately 85–90%.

riskFactors.factor: Age over 35 with lower urinary tract symptoms
riskFactors.description: Benign prostatic enlargement, urethral stricture, and incomplete bladder emptying predispose older men to ascending coliform infection; addressing these conditions reduces the risk.
Cystoscopy, catheter insertion, or prostate biopsy without adequate peri-procedural antimicrobial prophylaxis introduces bacteria into the urinary tract.
Immune compromise allows atypical organisms and more severe infection; CMV and Cryptococcus have been reported in HIV-positive men with epididymo-orchitis.
Men with a history of gonorrhea or chlamydia urethritis have higher rates of recurrent ascending infection, partly from incomplete prior treatment or ongoing exposure.
Congenital or acquired abnormalities — including posterior urethral valves, ectopic ureters, and bladder diverticula — impair drainage and raise susceptibility to ascending infection.

riskFactors.factor: Men who have sex with men (MSM)
riskFactors.description: MSM have higher rates of rectal gonorrhea and chlamydia as well as enteric organisms (E. coli, Haemophilus influenzae) reaching the urethra, elevating epididymo-orchitis risk.

affectedDemographics: Sexually active men aged 18–35, particularly those with new or multiple partners
Men over 50 with benign prostatic hyperplasia or other structural lower urinary tract pathology
Men who have sex with men
Men who have undergone recent urological procedures
Immunocompromised men, including those with HIV
Men in occupations or regions with brucellosis exposure
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical but requires urgent ultrasound to exclude testicular torsion, which cannot be reliably distinguished by history and examination alone. Colour Doppler scrotal ultrasound is the cornerstone investigation: epididymo-orchitis produces increased epididymal and testicular blood flow with surrounding oedema, whereas torsion shows absent or markedly reduced flow. Urethral swab or first-catch urine nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) is performed to identify chlamydia and gonorrhea. Mid-stream urine culture identifies coliform organisms, particularly in men over 35. Blood tests — full blood count and C-reactive protein — quantify systemic inflammation; blood cultures are added in febrile or systemically unwell patients. When TB or brucellosis is in the differential, early morning urine cultures for mycobacteria and Brucella serology are requested. Amiodarone levels are checked in patients on this drug. Key differential diagnoses include testicular torsion (emergency), isolated orchitis, testicular tumour, inguinal hernia extending into the scrotum, and Fournier gangrene (necrotising fasciitis of the genitalia). A secondary reactive hydrocele, visible on ultrasound, commonly accompanies epididymo-orchitis and usually resolves without separate treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes epididymo-orchitis (increased blood flow, epididymal enlargement, reactive hydrocele) from torsion (absent or asymmetrically reduced flow), and detects abscess.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Warm gel is applied to the scrotum; a probe is moved over the skin for 15–20 minutes. The procedure is painless, results are available immediately, and the radiologist will comment on blood flow and the presence of any fluid collections.

diagnosticTests.test: First-catch urine or urethral swab NAAT
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects chlamydia and gonorrhea DNA to confirm STI cause and guide targeted antibiotic therapy and partner notification.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The first 10–20 ml of urine passed on arrival is collected; a urethral swab (a small cotton-tipped stick gently inserted into the urethra) may also be taken. Results are available in 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies coliform organisms in men over 35 and provides antibiotic sensitivity data to guide step-down or targeted therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clean mid-stream urine sample is sent to the microbiology laboratory; culture results take 24–48 hours and sensitivity results 48–72 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies systemic inflammation and identifies bacteraemia in febrile or unwell patients to guide the need for intravenous antibiotics.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venepuncture; FBC and CRP are available within hours, blood cultures within 48–72 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Concurrent STI testing recommended whenever chlamydia or gonorrhea is confirmed, as co-infections significantly affect management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood drawn at the same sitting; results usually available within 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Repeat or extended scrotal ultrasound (abscess protocol)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Performed when symptoms do not improve after 72 hours of treatment to detect developing abscess or testicular infarction requiring surgical intervention.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same procedure as the initial ultrasound; the radiologist specifically assesses for fluid collections requiring drainage.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is guided by the patient's age, sexual behaviour, and likely causative organism, with empirical antibiotics started immediately pending results. In men under 35 where chlamydia and gonorrhea are probable, CDC, BASHH, and EAU guidelines recommend dual therapy: a single intramuscular dose of ceftriaxone 500 mg (covering gonorrhea) combined with oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10–14 days (covering chlamydia and providing additional Gram-negative coverage). Partner notification and treatment are a mandatory component of STI-related management. In men over 35 where coliforms are most likely, oral ofloxacin 200 mg twice daily or ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 14 days is recommended, adjusted once urine culture sensitivities are available. Both regimens are adjusted according to allergy, local resistance patterns, and renal function. Supportive care is integral: scrotal elevation using a supportive underwear or rolled towel, regular NSAIDs (ibuprofen or naproxen) for pain and oedema, and relative rest significantly improve comfort. The majority of patients can be managed in the outpatient setting; hospital admission is indicated for high fever, sepsis, persistent vomiting preventing oral therapy, or failure to respond within 72 hours. Intravenous ceftriaxone or gentamicin is used for admitted patients. Surgical intervention is reserved for confirmed abscess on ultrasound or non-viable testis at exploration. Follow-up at four to six weeks confirms resolution and assesses for complications including atrophy, chronic pain, or epididymal obstruction affecting fertility. If symptoms persist beyond three months, chronic epididymo-orchitis warrants reassessment for an underlying structural cause or unusual organism.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone (IM) + doxycycline
medicalTreatments.description: First-line dual antibiotic therapy for STI-related epididymo-orchitis in men under 35. Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose eliminates gonorrhea; doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10–14 days eliminates chlamydia and provides Gram-negative coverage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical resolution of pain and swelling in approximately 90–95% of chlamydial epididymo-orchitis; highly effective against susceptible gonorrhea strains.

medicalTreatments.name: Fluoroquinolones (ofloxacin or ciprofloxacin)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for coliform epididymo-orchitis in men over 35; ofloxacin 200 mg twice daily or ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 14 days. Also covers chlamydia; avoided as primary gonorrhea therapy because of resistance prevalence above 5% in many regions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microbiological cure in approximately 85–95% of susceptible coliform infections; clinical resolution parallels microbiological response.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-inflammatory analgesics reduce scrotal pain and oedema as essential adjunct therapy. Ibuprofen 400–600 mg three times daily or naproxen 500 mg twice daily during the acute phase; taken with food to reduce gastric side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant pain reduction in most patients within 24–48 hours; no impact on microbiological cure but markedly improves quality of life and adherence to treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous antibiotics (ceftriaxone IV, gentamicin)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for hospitalised patients with high fever, sepsis, vomiting, or oral treatment failure. Ceftriaxone 1–2 g daily IV or gentamicin with a β-lactam covers a broad spectrum pending culture results; stepped down to oral therapy once stable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Fever resolution in approximately 90% of hospitalised bacterial epididymo-orchitis patients within 48–72 hours of IV therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Scrotal elevation and ice packs
medicalTreatments.description: Elevating the scrotum continuously using a jockstrap and applying wrapped ice packs for 15–20 minutes several times daily in the acute phase improves lymphatic drainage and reduces oedema. Not a stand-alone treatment but consistently recommended as an adjunct in all clinical guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reported subjective pain reduction in the majority of patients; no high-quality trial data, but universally included in guideline-based management.

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage of epididymal or testicular abscess
surgicalOptions.description: When scrotal ultrasound confirms a localised pus collection, surgical drainage under general anaesthesia decompresses the abscess and allows microbiological sampling to guide targeted antibiotic therapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Abscess resolution and testicular preservation achieved in over 90% of cases when drainage is performed before extensive necrosis; minimally invasive aspiration may be possible for small unilocular collections.

surgicalOptions.name: Orchiectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the testis is reserved for cases with extensive necrosis, non-viable testicular tissue, or when a tumour cannot be excluded. Performed when drainage alone is insufficient or when significant testicular destruction has occurred.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive resolution of the infected focus in nearly 100% of cases; contralateral testis preserves fertility and androgen production in most patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Scrotal support underwear
alternativeTreatments.description: A supportive jockstrap or close-fitting underwear maintains the scrotum in an elevated position continuously, reducing gravitational pull on oedematous structures; an essential and cost-free adjunct.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sexual partner notification and treatment
alternativeTreatments.description: For STI-related cases, partner notification services offered through GUM clinics assist with contacting and treating partners, reducing re-infection and community STI burden.

alternativeTreatments.name: Increased fluid intake and rest
alternativeTreatments.description: Adequate hydration supports antibiotic clearance and renal function; bed rest during the acute phase reduces scrotal dependent oedema and discomfort.

whySeeSpecialist: A urologist must be involved when testicular torsion cannot be clinically excluded, requiring emergency Doppler ultrasound or surgical exploration. Specialist review is also warranted when scrotal ultrasound reveals an abscess or tumour, when the infection does not respond within 72 hours of appropriate antibiotic therapy, when both testes are involved, or when the patient has ongoing subfertility concerns. Genitourinary medicine (GUM) specialists manage STI-related epididymo-orchitis, ensuring complete STI screening, partner notification, and follow-up consistent with national guidelines.
doctorSelectionGuide: For acute presentations, attend an emergency department or genitourinary medicine (GUM or sexual health) clinic that has same-day scrotal Doppler ultrasound capability, as excluding testicular torsion is time-critical. For recurrent or chronic cases, a urologist with an interest in male reproductive health and access to cystoscopy, urodynamics, and fertility assessment provides the most comprehensive evaluation. Ask specifically about the clinic's approach to partner notification and STI co-screening.
preventionStrategies: Use barrier contraception (condoms) consistently to prevent chlamydia and gonorrhea transmission, the most common causes in young men.
Attend regular STI screening if you have multiple or new sexual partners, enabling treatment before ascending infection develops.
Treat urethritis promptly and completely; early antibiotic therapy prevents progression to epididymo-orchitis.
Ensure MMR vaccination with two doses to eliminate mumps-related risk.
Minimise unnecessary urological instrumentation and use antimicrobial prophylaxis when procedures are unavoidable.
Manage underlying urinary tract conditions — prostatic enlargement, stricture — that create ascending infection risk.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full antibiotic course without stopping early, even once pain improves.
Abstain from sexual intercourse until the full treatment course is finished and any sexual partners have been treated.
Wear a supportive jockstrap or snug underwear throughout the recovery period.
Avoid strenuous physical activity, cycling, or contact sports until pain and swelling have fully resolved.
Attend the follow-up appointment at four to six weeks; do not skip it even if you feel well, as silent complications can develop.

recommended: Adequate fluid intake (at least 1.5–2 litres per day) to support renal antibiotic clearance and general recovery
Anti-inflammatory foods — oily fish, leafy greens, berries — to support tissue healing
Probiotic foods (yoghurt, kefir) if taking prolonged antibiotics to reduce gastrointestinal side effects
avoid: Alcohol during antibiotic therapy, particularly with doxycycline (increases nausea and photosensitivity) and fluoroquinolones
Heavy lifting or activity that increases scrotal pressure and oedema during the acute phase

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is recommended during the acute phase when fever and significant scrotal pain are present. Once fever has resolved and pain is controlled — typically after 3–5 days of antibiotics — light walking is encouraged. Strenuous exercise, cycling, and contact sports should be avoided until all swelling and tenderness have resolved, which usually takes two to four weeks. Wearing a supportive jockstrap during any physical activity after the acute phase reduces discomfort.
dailyManagement: Take antibiotics at consistent times daily and complete the entire prescribed course.
Elevate the scrotum when resting by placing a rolled towel or folded pillow underneath.
Apply a wrapped ice pack (15–20 minutes, several times daily) during the first 48 hours.
Monitor for worsening pain, a new fluctuant swelling (suggesting abscess), or rising fever despite antibiotics.
Report promptly to a clinician if symptoms do not improve within 72 hours of starting treatment.
prognosis: The outlook for epididymo-orchitis treated promptly with appropriate antibiotics is generally excellent. The majority of men with STI-related disease experience complete resolution of pain and swelling within two to four weeks. Bacterial epididymo-orchitis from coliform organisms also responds well to antibiotics, though full resolution of the epididymal nodule may take several months. Testicular atrophy — the main long-term complication — occurs in approximately 10–40% of patients with significant acute disease, more commonly after mumps than bacterial epididymo-orchitis, and more commonly when treatment is delayed beyond 48–72 hours of symptom onset. Subfertility is rarely caused by unilateral disease because the contralateral testis compensates. Bilateral disease with atrophy can impair spermatogenesis and warrant a semen analysis six months after recovery. Chronic epididymal pain or an epididymal nodule affects a small proportion of men but usually does not affect function. With early treatment, permanent testicular loss or significant hormonal consequences are uncommon.
recoveryTimeline: Pain typically begins to ease within 2–3 days of starting effective antibiotics, and fever resolves in 2–4 days. Scrotal swelling takes longer to subside, usually one to three weeks. Full return to normal activity is expected within two to four weeks. Epididymal tenderness and a palpable thickening may persist for up to three months before fully resolving. A follow-up appointment at four to six weeks is standard to confirm resolution, assess for complications, and discuss fertility or recurrence concerns.
complications: Testicular or epididymal abscess — pus collection requiring drainage; occurs in approximately 3–8% of bacterial cases, most commonly when antibiotic therapy is delayed.
Testicular atrophy — reduction in testicular volume from ischaemia and inflammation; affects 10–40% of patients with significant acute epididymo-orchitis, particularly after mumps.
Subfertility — bilateral involvement or significant atrophy can impair spermatogenesis; a semen analysis six months after recovery quantifies the impact.
Chronic epididymitis — persistent epididymal pain or a palpable nodule lasting beyond three months, affecting quality of life in a minority of men.
Epididymal obstruction — fibrosis from recurrent or poorly treated infection can block sperm transport from the affected testis.
Fournier gangrene — a rare but life-threatening necrotising fasciitis of the genitalia and perineum that can complicate severe scrotal infection in immunocompromised or diabetic patients.

supportResources.name: NHS — Epididymo-orchitis
supportResources.description: NHS patient information on causes, symptoms, treatment and when to seek urgent care for epididymo-orchitis.

supportResources.name: CDC — Epididymitis STI Treatment Guidelines 2021
supportResources.description: U.S. Centers for Disease Control antibiotic treatment recommendations and clinical guidance for epididymitis.

supportResources.name: BASHH Epididymo-orchitis Guidelines
supportResources.description: British Association for Sexual Health and HIV clinical guideline for assessment and management of epididymo-orchitis.

sources.title: Street EJ et al. BASHH 2010 European guideline on the management of epididymo-orchitis. Int J STD AIDS 2011
Trojian TH et al. Epididymitis and Orchitis: An Overview. American Family Physician, 2009
NICE CKS — Epididymo-orchitis clinical knowledge summary 2022
Pilatz A et al. Acute epididymitis in ultrasound: results of a prospective study with baseline and follow-up investigations. Andrologia 2013

confusedWithConditions.name: Orchitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Isolated orchitis refers to inflammation of the testis only; epididymo-orchitis involves both the epididymis and testis. The distinction matters clinically because isolated orchitis is more often viral (mumps), while epididymo-orchitis is almost always bacterial.

confusedWithConditions.name: Syphilis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A syphilitic gumma of the epididymis or testis produces a painless, firm mass without fever or urethral discharge, closely mimicking a tumour rather than acute epididymo-orchitis; serological testing is diagnostic.

confusedWithConditions.name: Herpes simplex infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Genital herpes can cause scrotal tenderness and dysuria but produces characteristic painful vesicles or ulcers on the scrotal skin; it does not cause the internal epididymal tenderness and fever of epididymo-orchitis.

relatedConditions.name: Orchitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Orchitis — isolated testicular inflammation — is the most closely related entity; epididymo-orchitis is the combined form that develops when epididymitis spreads to the testis.

relatedConditions.name: Epididymitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Epididymitis is the precursor condition — isolated epididymal inflammation — that progresses to the combined epididymo-orchitis form in many cases.

relatedConditions.name: Gonorrhea
relatedConditions.relevance: Neisseria gonorrhoeae is a primary cause of STI-related epididymo-orchitis in sexually active men and requires specific dual-antibiotic treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Benign prostatic hyperplasia
relatedConditions.relevance: BPH creates urinary stasis and is the most common underlying condition predisposing older men to coliform epididymo-orchitis.

faqs.question: What is epididymo-orchitis?
faqs.answer: Epididymo-orchitis is inflammation of both the epididymis and testis at the same time. It is the most common cause of acute scrotal pain and swelling in adult men and is usually caused by a bacterial infection — most often chlamydia or gonorrhea in younger men, and gut bacteria in older men.

faqs.question: How is epididymo-orchitis different from testicular torsion?
faqs.answer: Both cause sudden scrotal pain, but torsion is a surgical emergency where the blood supply is cut off and the testis will die within hours without surgery. Epididymo-orchitis develops more gradually and is usually accompanied by fever or urethral symptoms. A colour Doppler ultrasound is needed urgently to distinguish the two — never wait to see if torsion resolves on its own.

faqs.question: Is epididymo-orchitis sexually transmitted?
faqs.answer: In men under 35 the most common cause is chlamydia or gonorrhea, which are sexually transmitted infections. In older men the cause is usually gut bacteria unrelated to sexual activity. Even when the cause is an STI, the condition itself is not directly transmitted to a partner — but the underlying STI can be, so partner treatment is essential.

faqs.question: What antibiotics treat epididymo-orchitis?
faqs.answer: For STI-related cases in men under 35, the recommended treatment is ceftriaxone 500 mg by injection plus doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10–14 days. For coliform cases in men over 35, ofloxacin or ciprofloxacin for 14 days is standard. The exact choice depends on age, local resistance patterns, and allergy history.

faqs.question: How long does epididymo-orchitis take to clear?
faqs.answer: Pain and fever typically improve within 2–3 days of starting antibiotics. Scrotal swelling takes 1–3 weeks to resolve. Full recovery and return to normal activity usually occur within 2–4 weeks. A palpable epididymal thickening may persist for several months.

faqs.question: Can epididymo-orchitis cause infertility?
faqs.answer: Unilateral epididymo-orchitis treated promptly rarely causes clinically significant infertility, as the contralateral testis compensates. Bilateral disease with atrophy or significant epididymal scarring can impair sperm production and transport. A semen analysis six months after recovery provides a definitive assessment if fertility is a concern.

faqs.question: When should I go to the emergency room?
faqs.answer: Go to the emergency room immediately if you have sudden severe testicular pain of abrupt onset, as this may be testicular torsion. Also seek urgent care for high fever with rigors, rapidly worsening scrotal swelling, a soft fluctuant area suggesting abscess, or symptoms that fail to improve after 48–72 hours of antibiotics.

faqs.question: Can I have sex during treatment for epididymo-orchitis?
faqs.answer: No. Abstain from sexual intercourse until the full antibiotic course is completed and all sexual partners have been tested and treated. For STI-related cases, resuming sex before partners are treated risks re-infection. Intercourse can also worsen discomfort during the acute phase.

faqs.question: What is the difference between epididymitis and epididymo-orchitis?
faqs.answer: Epididymitis is inflammation confined to the epididymis, the coiled tube behind the testis. When infection spreads from the epididymis into the testis itself, the combined condition is called epididymo-orchitis. The treatments are similar but epididymo-orchitis carries a higher risk of testicular atrophy.

faqs.question: Does epididymo-orchitis cause testicular shrinkage?
faqs.answer: Testicular atrophy — shrinkage — can occur in approximately 10–40% of cases with significant inflammation, particularly if treatment is delayed. Atrophy that persists beyond 2–3 months is usually permanent, though it rarely causes hormonal or fertility problems when only one testis is affected.

faqs.question: Do my sexual partners need treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes, if an STI such as chlamydia or gonorrhea was identified as the cause. Partners from the past 60 days should be tested and treated even if they have no symptoms, as asymptomatic STIs are common. A sexual health clinic can assist with partner notification confidentially.

faqs.question: Can epididymo-orchitis come back?
faqs.answer: Recurrent epididymo-orchitis occurs when an underlying cause is not addressed — for example, ongoing STI exposure without protection, untreated urinary outflow obstruction in older men, or incomplete antibiotic courses. Correcting these factors significantly reduces recurrence.

faqs.question: Is there a risk of abscess with epididymo-orchitis?
faqs.answer: An abscess — a collection of pus — develops in approximately 3–8% of bacterial cases, usually when treatment is delayed or incomplete. Signs include a soft, fluctuant area of the scrotum that does not improve with antibiotics. Ultrasound confirms abscess, which typically requires surgical drainage.

faqs.question: Can epididymo-orchitis be caused by a urinary tract infection?
faqs.answer: Yes. In men over 35, gut bacteria (usually E. coli) causing a urinary tract infection can ascend from the bladder through the vas deferens to the epididymis. Underlying conditions such as an enlarged prostate that cause urinary stasis make this more likely.

faqs.question: What tests diagnose epididymo-orchitis?
faqs.answer: A colour Doppler scrotal ultrasound confirms increased blood flow in the epididymis and testis and excludes torsion. Urine NAAT tests identify chlamydia and gonorrhea; urine culture identifies gut bacteria. Blood tests measure inflammation levels. STI serology (HIV, syphilis) is usually added.

faqs.question: How painful is epididymo-orchitis?
faqs.answer: Pain severity varies. Some men describe only a dull scrotal ache, while others find the pain severe enough to prevent walking. The epididymis and testis are among the most nerve-rich tissues in the body, making inflammation particularly uncomfortable. NSAIDs and scrotal elevation provide significant relief within 24–48 hours.

faqs.question: Can I treat epididymo-orchitis without antibiotics?
faqs.answer: No. Bacterial epididymo-orchitis requires antibiotics to clear the infection and prevent complications including abscess, atrophy, and subfertility. Supportive measures (NSAIDs, scrotal elevation, rest) help symptoms but do not eliminate the bacteria. Delaying antibiotic therapy worsens outcomes.

faqs.question: Is epididymo-orchitis the same as a varicocele?
faqs.answer: No. A varicocele is a painless collection of dilated veins around the testis that develops gradually; it does not cause fever or acute tenderness. Epididymo-orchitis is an acute bacterial infection with sudden-onset pain, swelling, and fever. An ultrasound distinguishes the two.

faqs.question: What follow-up is needed after epididymo-orchitis?
faqs.answer: A follow-up appointment at four to six weeks is recommended to confirm that the infection has fully cleared, check for epididymal nodules, assess testicular volume for atrophy, and discuss any concerns about fertility or recurrence. Earlier review is needed if symptoms worsen or do not improve within 72 hours of antibiotics.

faqs.question: Can epididymo-orchitis affect testosterone levels?
faqs.answer: Significant bilateral testicular damage from severe or repeated epididymo-orchitis can reduce testosterone production, causing symptoms of hypogonadism such as low libido, fatigue, and mood changes. This is uncommon after unilateral disease. Blood tests measuring testosterone and gonadotrophins confirm the diagnosis.

metaDescription: Epididymo-orchitis inflames the epididymis and testis causing scrotal pain, swelling and fever. Learn STI causes, antibiotic treatment and when to seek care.
